steps.text: Conduct outreach to keep Indian Tribes informed about the agency's actions and plans.
Provide Tribes with information about the Commission's mission and regulatory authority, and identify opportunities for Tribal involvement.
Invite Tribal input throughout the decision making processes.

preconditions.text: Regulatory actions have substantial direct effects on one or more Indian Tribes.

steps.text: Do not consider interactions with Tribal government officials only from your own cultural perspective.
Be sensitive to the cultural perspectives of all persons with whom you interrelate.
Recognize important Tribal priorities.

constraints.text: Tribal representatives may seem to place less priority than Federal employees do on timelines, schedules, and agendas established by U.S. Government agencies.

steps.text: Develop comprehensive planning schedules and communication plans to provide opportunities for Tribal involvement.
Consider environmental impacts in decision making.
Inform the public of decisions.
Document the process by which the agency made its informed decision.
Consider the effects on historic properties of certain projects that are carried out, assisted, funded, permitted, licensed, or approved.
Afford the ACHP a reasonable opportunity to comment on such undertakings.

constraints.text: Comply with the NRC's regulatory requirements in 10 CFR Part 51.

steps.text: Examine the applicant's inadvertent discovery procedures to determine whether they comply with NAGPRA.
Initiate dialogue through written correspondence announcing a regulatory action that may have Tribal implications.
Send copies of correspondence relating to NEPA or NHPA actions to the Tribal Historic Preservation Officer (THPO) or equivalent staff.
Conduct a follow-up meeting with Tribal officials or representatives.

preconditions.text: There is an evaluation of an application from a Federal applicant or a voluntary private applicant.
Expressions of interest are received from individual Tribes.

constraints.text: Correspondence should be between the leader of the Tribal government and an appropriate representative of NRC management.
Initial correspondence must describe the proposed regulatory action and identify the location and methods for the Tribe to provide comments or request additional information.
For actions related to NEPA or the NHPA, the correspondence must include information on the environmental review and NHPA Section 106 processes and an invitation to participate.

steps.text: Seek the views of the appropriate SHPO when identifying historic properties.
Evaluate the eligibility of historic properties for the National Register of Historic Places.
Assess effects of an undertaking on historic properties.
Consult with SHPOs when developing MOAs and PAs under NHPA Section 106.
Give participants copies of the transcript and the opportunity to provide written corrections in the record.
Seek clarification of the issues and work to resolve the differences.
Summarize the topics discussed and any commitments made and identify outstanding issues.
Follow the general agency correspondence procedures for written correspondence sent to a Tribal representative.
Follow up with a telephone call to the Tribal contact.
Provide time for people to speak without interruption.

preconditions.text: Parties dispute or disagree on the substance of a meeting summary
A reply is not received within the expected time period

constraints.text: Tribal elders, in particular, are afforded great respect when speaking.

steps.text: Provide time for people to speak without interruption during meetings.
Contact the Office of Nuclear Material Safety and Safeguards (NMSS) Federal, State, and Tribal Liaison Branch for assistance in arranging for translators.
Address Tribal leaders by their official titles and names.
Introduce Tribal leaders before other participants and invite them to address the meeting first.
Display respect for the Tribe’s customs and beliefs if a representative opens the meeting with a prayer, song, or invocation.
Limit promises and commitments to those within your authority or the agency’s statutory authority.
Present agency views, opinions, materials, and ideas honestly and openly, clearly distinguishing them from personal viewpoints.
Limit discussions to subjects that fall within the NRC’s statutory authority.

preconditions.text: If Tribal members, especially Tribal elders, appreciate the option of having an interpreter present at the meetings.

constraints.text: Do not expect to convene a meeting immediately at the appointed time and recognize that the start may be delayed until all participants are assembled.
Understand that working through an agenda may not be important to all participants.

steps.text: Consider the prior experience, involvement, education, and training of the Tribe as related to the technical or licensing matter at hand.
Determine the need to provide explanations for technical and licensing terms.
Respect Tribal interests, cultural diversity, customs, and agendas.
Respect Tribal Council leaders as officials of another government.
Listen carefully; ask questions for clarification.
Consider what you say; do not promise what you may be unable to deliver.
Communicate with the Tribe because it is the key to successful relationships.
Familiarize yourself with the Tribe’s specific information and understand the history and current issues, concerns, and expertise of the Tribe or Tribes with whom you may work.

constraints.text: When beginning an important discussion or when responding to questions by Tribal elders or officials, inappropriate laughter may be considered a lack of respect or seriousness or belittling the subject matter.
The NRC staff should dress respectfully as for any business meeting with elected officials or distinguished representatives of a government.
